topic.name: Video Game Development and Genres
topic.description: The documents discuss various video games across different genres and platforms, highlighting their development, gameplay mechanics, and reception.

1: The Battle For Midway is a turn-based strategy video game developed and published by Personal Software Services.
The game is set during the Battle of Midway in the Pacific Ocean theatre of World War II.
2: Indiana Jones and the Temple of Doom is an action game released in 1988 for the Nintendo Entertainment System.
The game is based on the arcade game and film of the same name.
3: Ratchet & Clank: Going Commando is a 2003 3D platform video game developed by Insomniac Games.
The gameplay of Going Commando is similar to that of the original Ratchet & Clank.
4: Myst is a franchise centered on a series of adventure video games.
The player takes the role of an unnamed person referred to as the Stranger and assists Atrus by traveling to other Ages and solving puzzles.
5: Grand Theft Auto IV is an open world action-adventure video game developed by Rockstar North.
The open world design lets players freely roam Liberty City.
6: The seventh generation includes consoles released since late 2005 by Nintendo, Microsoft, and Sony Computer Entertainment.
Each new console introduced a new type of breakthrough in technology.
7: Caesars Palace 2000 is a gambling simulation video game developed by Runecraft and published by Interplay Entertainment.
It is named after the famous Caesars Palace luxury hotel and casino on the Las Vegas Strip.

topic.name: Political Movements and Civil Liberties
topic.description: This topic encompasses the influence of political movements, civil liberties, and the role of individuals in shaping political landscapes across different historical contexts.

Document 1: Vladimír Remek is a Czech politician and diplomat as well as a former cosmonaut and military pilot.
Remek was a member of the European Parliament between 2004 and 2013.
Document 2: Anarchism as a social movement in Cuba held great influence with the working classes during the 19th and early 20th century.
Cuban anarchism mainly took the form of anarcho-collectivism based on the works of Mikhail Bakunin.
Document 4: The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) is a nonpartisan, non-profit organization whose stated mission is to defend and preserve the individual rights and liberties guaranteed to every person in this country by the Constitution and laws of the United States.
The ACLU has over 500,000 members and has an annual budget of over $100 million.

topic.name: Cultural and Artistic Expressions
topic.description: The documents explore various forms of cultural and artistic expressions, including literature, philosophy, and visual arts, highlighting their historical contexts and significance.

Document 1: The story tells of young Belgian reporter Tintin and his dog Snowy, who travel to Morocco to pursue a gang of international opium smugglers.
The Crab with the Golden Claws was adapted for the 1956 Belvision Studios animation Hergé's Adventures of Tintin.
Document 2: Pound uses this anecdote to emphasize an empirical approach for learning about art, in contrast to relying on commentary rooted in abstraction.
The parable has been used to illustrate the benefits of scientific thinking.
Document 3: Chrysippus expanded the fundamental doctrines of Zeno of Citium, the founder of the school.
He adhered to a deterministic view of fate, but nevertheless sought a role for personal freedom in thought and action.
Document 4: A prayer, 'God Help the Outcasts' is a somber hymn in which a beset Esmeralda asks God to shield outcasts and gypsies like herself against racism and discrimination.
The song also establishes Esmeralda as a selfless, empathetic character with whom Quasimodo falls in love.
Document 5: The scene was probably inspired by Alexander Pope's satirical poem The Dunciad.
It depicts a scene in a small, dingy attic room where a poet sits at his desk in the dormer and, scratching his head, stares at the papers on the desk before him.
Document 6: The Irenaean theodicy is a Christian theodicy designed to respond to the problem of evil.
It defends the probability of an omnipotent and omnibenevolent God in the face of evidence of evil in the world.
Document 7: Akhmatova's work ranges from short lyric poems to intricately structured cycles, such as Requiem, her tragic masterpiece about the Stalinist terror.
Her writing can be said to fall into two periods – the early work and her later work, divided by a decade of reduced literary output.

topic.name: Tropical Cyclones and Hurricanes
topic.description: The documents discuss the formation, development, and impact of various tropical cyclones and hurricanes across different years and regions.

Document 1: Hurricane Darby was the fourth named storm of the 1992 Pacific hurricane season.
The storm remained well offshore, although it killed three people.
Document 2: Tropical Storm Danny was a weak and disorganized tropical cyclone that formed in August 2009.
Danny had only minor effects on land.
Document 4: The 1942 Belize hurricane was the only known hurricane to strike Belize in the month of November.
This storm was detected in the vicinity of Turks and Caicos Islands on November 5.
Document 5: Hurricane Fran was a long-tracked Category 1 hurricane that caused little damage during its existence.
Originating from a tropical wave that developed off the coast of Hispaniola on October 1.
Document 6: Tropical Storm Felice was a modest tropical cyclone that lightly impacted parts of the Gulf Coast of the United States.
The effects from Felice were generally light.
Document 7: Hurricane Ella brought flooding to the Greater Antilles and Texas in September 1958.
Flooding in that country killed 30 people in Aux Cayes.

Document 2: The Howmet TX was an American sports prototype racing car designed in 1968.
It was the first turbine to win a race.
Document 3: The 2010 Heluva Good! Sour Cream Dips 400 was a NASCAR Sprint Cup Series stock car race.

The championship was contested over twenty rounds.

Lewis Hamilton won the race after starting from pole position.
Document 6: The 2015 Brazilian Grand Prix was a Formula One motor race held at the Autódromo José Carlos Pace.
Nico Rosberg won the race from his teammate.
Document 7: Maurício Gugelmin is a former racing driver from Brazil.
He participated in both Formula One and the Champ Car World Series.

topic.name: The Boat Race History
topic.description: A historical overview of the annual rowing competition between the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge, known as The Boat Race, detailing its significance, outcomes, and notable events since its inception in 1829.

Document 1: The Boat Race is a side-by-side rowing competition between the University of Oxford and the University of Cambridge.
The race was first held in 1829, and since 1845 has taken place on the 4.2-mile Championship Course on the River Thames.
Document 2: The Boat Race is a side-by-side rowing race between crews from the Universities of Oxford and Cambridge along the River Thames.
First held in 1829, the race takes place on the 4.2-mile Championship Course on the River Thames in southwest London.

topic.name: Historic Castles in Britain
topic.description: The documents discuss various historic castles in Britain, their architectural features, historical significance, and current status.

Document 1: Loch Leven Castle is a ruined castle on an island in Loch Leven, in the Perth and Kinross local authority area of Scotland.
Mary, Queen of Scots was imprisoned here in 1567 – 1568.
Document 2: Worcester Castle was a Norman fortification built between 1068 and 1069.
The castle played an important part in the wars of the 12th and early 13th century.
Document 3: St Briavels Castle is a moated Norman castle at St Briavels in the English county of Gloucestershire.
The castle became first a favourite hunting lodge of King John.
Document 6: Peckforton Castle is a Victorian country house built in the style of a medieval castle.
The house was built in the middle of the 19th century as a family home for John Tollemache.

topic.name: Military Leaders in Ancient and Medieval History
topic.description: The documents discuss significant military leaders and conflicts from ancient and medieval history, focusing on their campaigns, strategies, and impacts on their respective regions.

1: Alexander the Great was a King of the Ancient Greek kingdom of Macedon and is considered one of history's most successful military commanders.
He spent most of his ruling years on an unprecedented military campaign through Asia and northeast Africa.
2: John Doukas was a senior military figure of his reign and secured the imperial possessions in the western Balkans against the Serbs.
He scoured the Aegean of the fleets of the Turkish emir Tzachas.
3: Constantine Diogenes was a prominent Byzantine general active in the Balkans.
He served with distinction in the final stages of the Byzantine conquest of Bulgaria.
4: The Fourth Fitna involved conflict between the brothers al-Amin and al-Ma'mun over the succession to the throne of the Abbasid Caliphate.
Al-Ma'mun sought the support of the provincial élites of Khurasan and made moves to assert his own autonomy.
5: Pyrrhus' invasion of the Peloponnese was opposed by Macedon and a coalition of Greek city-states, most notably Sparta.
The war ended in a joint victory by Macedonia and Sparta.
6: The Battle of Boroughbridge was fought between rebellious barons and King Edward II of England, resulting in Lancaster's defeat and execution.
The battle represented significant steps in military developments with the extensive use of foot soldiers.
7: Izz ad-Din al-Qassam was a Syrian Muslim preacher and leader in the local struggles against British and French Mandatory rule.
He formed bands of local fighters and launched attacks against British and Jewish targets.

topic.name: Political Figures in North America
topic.description: This cluster discusses notable political figures from the United States and Canada, highlighting their careers, contributions, and political contexts.

Document 1: Richard Neal is an American politician and the U.S. Representative for Massachusetts's 1st congressional district.
He is a member of the Democratic Party and a former city councilor and mayor of Springfield, Massachusetts.
Document 2: Norma Paulus is an American lawyer and politician in the state of Oregon.
She became the first woman to hold a statewide elected office in Oregon when she became Oregon Secretary of State in 1977.
Document 3: Cornelius Gallagher was a meat merchant and politician in Alberta, Canada.
He served as a municipal councillor and briefly as the third mayor of Edmonton.
Document 4: John Hugill was a Canadian lawyer and politician who served as Attorney General of the province of Alberta from 1935 until 1937.
He became a prominent resident of Calgary and served two years on its city council.
Document 7: Bill Clinton is an American politician who was the 42nd President of the United States from 1993 to 2001.
Clinton was previously Governor of Arkansas and a member of the Democratic Party.

topic.name: State and Provincial Highways
topic.description: The documents discuss various state and provincial highways in North America, detailing their routes, history, and significance.

Document 1: Ontario Highway 80 was a provincially maintained highway in the Canadian province of Ontario.
Highway 80 was first designated in 1937, travelling east from Highway 79 in Alvinston to Highway 2 in Strathburn.
Document 2: Maryland Route 70 is a state highway in the U.S. state of Maryland.
MD 70 was constructed in the mid-1950s contemporaneously with the US 50 freeway.
Document 3: M-212 is a state trunkline highway in the US state of Michigan.
M-212 was assigned on December 29, 1937, from the intersection with Second Street to an intersection with US Highway 23.
Document 4: State Route 305 is a 13.50-mile-long state highway in the U.S. state of Washington.
SR 305 was created during the 1964 highway renumbering.
Document 5: The River Road National Scenic Byway is a National Scenic Byway and National Forest Scenic Byway in the US state of Michigan.
The section of the River Road that follows M-65 was added to the State Trunkline Highway System in the 1930s.
Document 6: King's Highway 406 is a 400-series highway in the Canadian province of Ontario.
Construction of Highway 406 began in 1963.
Document 7: U.S. Route 50 in California runs east from I-80 in West Sacramento to the Nevada state line.
Much of US 50 was constructed during the initial construction of the California state highway system.

Most families of entoprocts are colonial, and all but 2 of the 150 species are marine.

It is parasitic on oak and various other hardwood species.

It is known from Europe, Asia, and North America.
Document 5: Amanita atkinsoniana is a species of fungus in the Amanitaceae family
It grows solitarily or in small groups on the ground in mixed woods.
Document 6: The indigo bunting is a small seed-eating bird in the family Cardinalidae
It migrates from southern Canada to northern Florida during the breeding season.
Document 7: Ascocoryne sarcoides is a species of fungus in the family Helotiaceae
This common fungus appears as a gelatinous mass of pinkish or purple-colored discs.

topic.name: Historical and Natural Sites
topic.description: The documents discuss various historical and natural sites, highlighting their significance and characteristics.

Document 1: Apple River Fort State Historic Site was one of many frontier forts hastily completed by settlers in northern Illinois and southern Wisconsin following the onset of the 1832 Black Hawk War.
Today, a replica of the fort stands next to the site of the original Apple River Fort.
Document 2: Muncy Creek is a tributary of the West Branch Susquehanna River in Sullivan County and Lycoming County, in Pennsylvania.
The area in its vicinity was settled in 1783.
Document 3: Horton Plains National Park in Ohiya is a protected area in the central highlands of Sri Lanka and is covered by montane grassland and cloud forest.
This region was designated a national park in 1988.
Document 4: Kaimanawa horses are a population of feral horses in New Zealand that are descended from domestic horses released in the 19th and 20th centuries.
The herd was protected by the New Zealand government in 1981.
Document 5: Ben Nevis is the highest mountain in the British Isles, located in Scotland.
The mountain is a popular destination, attracting an estimated 100,000 ascents a year.
Document 6: The Anglo-Norman horse is a warmblood horse breed developed in Lower Normandy in northern France.
The remaining types were eventually standardized, although there remained some criticism of the 'hybrid' nature of the breed's conformation.
Document 7: Becket Hill State Park Reserve is a public recreation area lying adjacent to Nehantic State Forest in the town of Lyme, Connecticut, United States.
The park's name is sometimes misspelled as Beckett Hill.

topic.name: Military History and Engineering
topic.description: The documents discuss various military figures and events from different historical periods, highlighting their contributions and roles in warfare and engineering.

1: Leif Johan Sverdrup was a Norwegian-born American civil engineer and general with the U.S. Army Corps of Engineers during World War II.
He is best known for his service in the Southwest Pacific Area during World War II.
2: The Battle of Torrence 's Tavern was a minor engagement of the American Revolutionary War.
The engagement took place on either February 1 or February 2, 1781 immediately following the Battle of Cowan 's Ford.
3: Brigadier Stanley James Ledger Hill served as commander of the 3rd Parachute Brigade during World War II.
He commanded the battalion during its first airborne operation in North Africa.
4: Eric Stanley Lock was a British Royal Air Force fighter pilot and flying ace of the Second World War.
Lock became the RAF 's most successful Allied pilot during the Battle of Britain.
5: The 29th Regiment Massachusetts Volunteer Infantry was an infantry regiment in the Union army during the American Civil War.
The regiment took part in 29 battles and four sieges in a variety of theaters of the war.
6: The Dalj massacre was the killing of 56 or 57 Croats during the Croatian War of Independence.
The killings were extensively covered by the International Criminal Tribunal for the former Yugoslavia.
7: Hastati were a class of infantry in the armies of the early Roman Republic.
They were originally some of the poorest men in the legion, and could afford only modest equipment.

contains works by three American 20th-century composers

features ten covers of soul classics

Ocean's songwriting touches on themes such as unrequited love, decadence, class, and drugs
Document 5: Free is the second album by American progressive rock band OSI
was met with generally positive critical reception
Document 6: Deep Blue is a song by English musician George Harrison
the composition was inspired by the deteriorating condition of his mother
Document 7: Sie werden aus Saba alle kommen is a church cantata by Johann Sebastian Bach
Bach wrote the cantata to conclude his first Christmas season as Thomaskantor in Leipzig

topic.name: Basketball and Football Athletes
topic.description: This cluster discusses notable athletes in basketball and football, highlighting their careers, achievements, and contributions to their respective sports.

1: Pau Gasol is a Spanish professional basketball player for the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
He has won two NBA championships, both with the Los Angeles Lakers.
4: Manu Ginóbili is an Argentine professional basketball player for the San Antonio Spurs of the National Basketball Association (NBA).
He has earned four NBA championships and was named an All-Star in 2005 and 2011.
3: David Molk is a former American football center.
He played professionally in the National Football League (NFL) for the Philadelphia Eagles, and the San Diego Chargers.
6: Thomas Wilcher is a high school athletic coach and teacher who was previously a National Collegiate Athletic Association (NCAA) Division I track and field national champion.
He was also a running back for the Michigan Wolverines football team from 1983 – 1986.
2: The 1984 Independence Bowl was a post-season American college football bowl game between the Virginia Tech Hokies and the Air Force Falcons.
The game ended in a 23 – 7 victory for the Air Force Academy.
5: The 1957 NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship Game took place on March 23, 1957 between the North Carolina Tar Heels and Kansas Jayhawks.
The match-up was the final one of the nineteenth consecutive NCAA Men's Division I Basketball Championship.
7: The 2010 – 11 Harvard Crimson men's basketball team represented Harvard University in the Ivy League athletic conference during the 2010 – 11 NCAA Division I men's basketball season.
The team became the first men's basketball Ivy League champion in school history.

topic.name: World War II Aircraft Development
topic.description: This cluster discusses various aircraft developed and used during World War II, focusing on their design, production, and operational history.

1: The Yermolayev Yer-2 was a long-range Soviet medium bomber used during World War II.
Production was terminated in August 1941 to allow the factory to concentrate on building higher-priority Ilyushin Il-2 ground-attack aircraft.
2: The Ryan FR Fireball was a mixed-power fighter aircraft designed by Ryan Aeronautical for the United States Navy during World War II.
Only 66 aircraft were built before Japan surrendered in August 1945.
3: The Tupolev Tu-85 was a Soviet prototype strategic bomber based on the Tu-4, an unlicensed reverse engineered copy of the Boeing B-29 Superfortress.
Only two prototypes were built before the program was canceled in favor of the Tupolev Tu-95 bomber.
4: The Vought F6U Pirate was the Vought company's first jet fighter, designed for US Navy during the mid-1940s.
None were ever issued to operational squadrons and they were relegated to development, training and test roles.
5: The Lockheed AH-56 Cheyenne was an attack helicopter developed by Lockheed for the United States Army.
The AH-56's maiden flight took place on 21 September 1967.
6: No. 482 Squadron was a maintenance unit of the Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) formed in May 1942.
Over the years it serviced the wing's Consolidated B-24 Liberators, Avro Lincolns, English Electric Canberras.
7: No. 84 Wing is a Royal Australian Air Force (RAAF) transport wing formed in 1944 for army co-operation duties in the South West Pacific theatre of World War II.
It was re-formed in 1991 as a tactical transport wing headquartered at RAAF Base Townsville.

topic.name: Mathematics and Physics Experiments
topic.description: This cluster encompasses various significant experiments and concepts in mathematics and physics, illustrating foundational principles and theories.

1: Penrose tilings are named after mathematician and physicist Roger Penrose.
Penrose tilings are simple examples of aperiodic tilings of the plane.
4: The Fizeau experiment was carried out by Hippolyte Fizeau in 1851 to measure the relative speeds of light in moving water.
Einstein later pointed out the importance of the experiment for special relativity.
6: The Schiehallion experiment was an 18th-century experiment to determine the mean density of the Earth.
The experiment involved measuring the tiny deflection of a pendulum due to the gravitational attraction of a nearby mountain.
7: The Mayer – Vietoris sequence is an algebraic tool to help compute algebraic invariants of topological spaces.
The result is due to two Austrian mathematicians, Walther Mayer and Leopold Vietoris.

topic.name: Transportation Infrastructure
topic.description: This topic encompasses various forms of transportation infrastructure including railways, tunnels, and canals, highlighting their construction, operation, and historical significance.

Document 1: Nailsea and Backwell railway station, on the Bristol to Exeter Line, is in the village of Backwell
It is managed by Great Western Railway, the seventh company to be responsible for the station
Document 3: The Bømlafjord Tunnel is a subsea road tunnel under Bømlafjorden which connects the island of Føyno
The tunnel is the longest subsea tunnel in Norway
Document 6: The Anglesey Central Railway was a 17.5 miles long standard-gauge railway in Anglesey, Wales
Passenger services were withdrawn in 1964 as part of the Beeching Axe
Document 7: The Grantham Canal is a canal that runs for 33 miles from Grantham
It was built primarily to allow for the transportation of coal to Grantham

topic.name: Science Fiction Television Episodes
topic.description: The documents discuss various episodes from popular science fiction and drama television series, highlighting their plots, character developments, and viewer reception.

Document 1: 'Tempus Fugit' is the seventeenth episode of the fourth season of the American science fiction television series The X-Files.
The episode helped to explore the overarching mythology, or fictional history of The X-Files.
Document 3: 'Chinga' is the tenth episode of the fifth season of the American science fiction television series The X-Files.
The show centers on FBI special agents Fox Mulder and Dana Scully who work on cases linked to the paranormal, called X-Files.
Document 6: 'The Big Bang' is the thirteenth and final episode in the fifth series of British science fiction television programme Doctor Who.
It is the second part of the two-part series finale started with 'The Pandorica Opens'.
Document 7: 'The Cure' is the sixth episode of the first season of the American science fiction drama television series Fringe.
The episode followed two women suffering from a fictional disease.

topic.name: Naval Warships of Early 20th Century
topic.description: This topic encompasses the design, service history, and operational roles of various naval warships from the late 19th to early 20th century, focusing on their contributions during significant conflicts such as World War I.

Document 1: Imperator Aleksandr III was the third, and last, ship of the Imperatritsa Mariya-class dreadnoughts of the Imperial Russian Navy.
She was completed in 1917 and saw service with the Black Sea Fleet.
Document 2: Chanzy was an Amiral Charner-class armored cruiser built for the French Navy in the 1890s.
She served in the Mediterranean Squadron and was assigned to the International Squadron off the island of Crete during the Greco-Turkish War of 1897.
Document 3: Admiral Makarov was the second of the four Bayan-class armored cruisers built for the Imperial Russian Navy during the mid-1900s.
The ship fought several inconclusive battles with German ships during the war, including the Battle of Åland Islands in mid-1915.
Document 4: SMS Preussen was an armored frigate of the German Kaiserliche Marine, commissioned into the fleet in July 1876.
She cruised the Mediterranean Sea several times during her service.
Document 5: The Bussard class of unprotected cruisers were built for the German Kaiserliche Marine in the late 1880s and early 1890s.
They were designed for service in Germany's colonial empire.
Document 6: HMS Achilles was a Warrior-class armoured cruiser built for the Royal Navy in the first decade of the 20th century.
She served with the 2nd Cruiser Squadron for most of the First World War.
Document 7: HMS Boadicea was the lead ship of her class of scout cruisers built for the Royal Navy in the first decade of the 20th century.
She was present at, but did not fight in, the Battle of Jutland in mid-1916.

topic.name: Professional Wrestling Promotions
topic.description: The documents discuss various aspects of professional wrestling, focusing on events, championships, and notable personalities within promotions like TNA and WWE.

Document 1: Shelly Martinez is known for her work with World Wrestling Entertainment (WWE) on its ECW brand under the ring name Ariel and for Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) under the ring name Salinas.
Martinez entered professional wrestling in December 2000.
Document 2: Bound for Glory IV was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by the Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) promotion.
The show was promoted as TNA's premier PPV event and their equivalent to WWE's WrestleMania.
Document 4: The CMLL World Middleweight Championship is a professional wrestling world championship promoted by the Mexican wrestling promotion Consejo Mundial de Lucha Libre (CMLL).
The title is awarded to a wrestler after he 'wins' a match to maintain the illusion that professional wrestling is a competitive sport.
Document 5: Bound for Glory (2005) was a professional wrestling pay-per-view (PPV) event produced by the Total Nonstop Action Wrestling (TNA) promotion.
It was the first event under the Bound for Glory name and tenth event in the 2005 TNA PPV schedule.

topic.name: Historical Figures and Contributions
topic.description: This cluster discusses notable historical figures and their significant contributions in various fields such as medicine, journalism, military service, and science.

Document 1: Charles McKnight was an American physician during and after the American Revolutionary War.
He served as a surgeon and physician in the Hospital Department of the Continental Army.
Document 2: Helen Rollason was a British sports journalist and television presenter.
She became the first female presenter of the BBC's sports programme Grandstand.
Document 3: John Hines was a British-born Australian soldier of World War I.
He gained fame for the collection of items that he amassed during the war.
Document 4: Robert F. Christy was a Canadian-American theoretical physicist and later astrophysicist.
He was one of the last surviving people to have worked on the Manhattan Project.
Document 5: Halotus was a servant to the Roman Emperor Claudius and a suspect in his murder.
He served as a taster and chief steward to Claudius.
Document 6: Charles Boycott was an English land agent whose ostracism gave the English language the verb 'to boycott'.
He worked as a land agent for Lord Erne in County Mayo.
Document 7: Dorothy McKibbin worked on the Manhattan Project during World War II.
She was known as the 'first lady of Los Alamos' and ran the project's office.
